title: Negative temperature coefficient of Gilbert damping in magnetic bilayers
authors: Lulu Cao, Yuting Gong, Xianyang Lu, Yongbing Xu, Ya Zhai, Jing Wu, Roy W. Chantrell, Richard F. L. Evans
abstract: The Gilbert damping of magnetic materials is an important magnetic parameter that determines the switching speed and energy dissipation of spintronic devices. In simple metals, the intrinsic Gilbert damping increases with temperature and diverges near the Curie temperature as a result of spin fluctuations. Here we present atomistic simulations and experimental measurements showing surprising and opposite behavior in Py/Nd bilayers, where the Gilbert damping decreases with increasing temperature. The effect arises because of the enhanced damping at the interface as a result of spin pumping, where elevated temperatures cause a dynamic separation of the interfacial and bulk magnetization during relaxation. Furthermore, the temperature dependence of the damping can be controlled by varying the thickness of the Nd capping layer. Our findings present a new spintronic effect that can be used to modify the dynamic properties of nanoscale materials and devices for enhanced energy efficiency or with improved switching dynamics.
summary: This work explores the temperature dependence of Gilbert damping in magnetic bilayers, finding a surprising negative temperature coefficient in Py/Nd systems. The effect is explained by interface-enhanced damping due to spin pumping, which weakens as temperature rises. This offers a new mechanism to control the dynamic properties of spintronic materials.
main_problem: The paper investigates the unusual temperature dependence of the Gilbert damping in magnetic bilayers, specifically observing a decrease in damping as temperature increases in Py/Nd systems.
main_result: The damping exhibits a negative temperature coefficient in Py/Nd bilayers, which is attributed to interface-enhanced damping effects from spin pumping that diminish at higher temperatures.
method: The study combines atomistic spin dynamics simulations using the sLLG equation with complementary time-resolved magneto-optical Kerr effect (TR-MOKE) measurements.
model_or_system: The physical system is magnetic bilayers, such as Py/Nd, modeled using a classical spin Hamiltonian and governed by the stochastic Landau–Lifshitz–Gilbert (sLLG) equation. The interface damping enhancement due to spin pumping is a key physical mechanism.
key_observables: Effective Gilbert damping ($\alpha_{	ext{eff}}$), relaxation time ($	au$), and precession frequency ($f$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($T$), Nd capping layer thickness ($d_{	ext{NM}}$), and the magnitude of interface spin accumulation.
main_assumptions: The sign of the temperature dependence of $\alpha_{	ext{eff}}$ depends critically on the magnitude of the interface spin accumulation (large accumulation $ightarrow$ negative coefficient).
figures_summary: Figures show simulated and measured $\alpha_{	ext{eff}}$ vs. temperature, demonstrating the systematic decrease in damping for Py/Nd bilayers compared to the expected behavior in pure films.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by modeling spin dynamics and then presents results comparing the temperature dependence of damping in different bilayer structures ($	ext{Py}/	ext{Nd}$ vs. $	ext{Pt}/	ext{Py}$), contrasting the mechanisms responsible for the observed sign change.
why_interesting: The study provides a clear example of how interface effects and thermal dynamics can lead to non-monotonic or counter-intuitive behavior in magnetic damping, which is relevant to understanding energy dissipation in nanoscale spintronic devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic view of the simulated Py (6 nm)/Nd (t nm) bi- layer. The structural dimensions are 20×20×(6+t)nm3, where the dark grey underlayer and the red inserted layer represent the bulk and interface Py layer, respectively. The light-grey top layer represents the Nd layer.

FIG. 2. Simulated time evolution of the magnetisation trace (points) for the Py (6 nm)/Nd (32 nm) bilayer at different temperatures (a) T = 0 K, (b) T = 300 K, (c) T = 800 K showing a systematic de- crease in the effective Gilbert damping αeff with increasing tempera- ture as seen from the amplitude of the envelope function depicted by the shaded region. The lines are fitted to the data using Equation 3.

FIG. 3. Simulated mean-effective Gilbert damping as a function of temperature for Py (6 nm)/Nd (0, 1, 8, 16, 24, 32 nm) bilayers. The data show a consistent decrease of the effective damping with tem- perature for Py (6 nm)/Nd (8, 16, 24, 32 nm) bilayers, and the ex- pected increase for the case for pure Py.
FIG. 4. Experimentally measured effective Gilbert damping as a function of the pump fluence of the laser IF for Nd (t = 0, 8, 12, 16 nm)/Py (6 nm) bilayers.
